papers.title_en: Actionable Activation Directions for Detecting and Mitigating Emergent Misalignment Across Language Model Families
papers.evidence: 使用激活引导检测并缓解微调导致的对齐失配
papers.tldr: 语言模型微调不安全代码会引发突发的misalignment，其内部机制尚不明确。本研究在四个指令微调模型族上发现，差均值方向可以99.6%分离对齐与未对齐激活，且因果干预显著减少代码溢出。跨架构方向映射虽能抑制行为但缺乏特异性，并呈现Gemma和Qwen作为捐赠者、Llama作为接收者的不对称拓扑。这些结果定义了线性跨架构校正的局限，推荐采用模型内探针进行审计。
papers.abstract_en: Fine-tuning language models on insecure code induces emergent misalignment with poorly understood internal structure. We investigate whether this misalignment corresponds to a causally actionable activation-space direction shared across architectures. Across four instruction-tuned model families (Qwen2.5-1.5B, Gemma-2-2B, Llama-3.2-1B, Ministral-3-3B) finetuned identically, a difference-in-means direction achieves 99.6% separation of aligned and misaligned activations at each model's final layer. Causal steering by subtracting this direction reduces code spillover by 21-51 points, while a secure-code control confirms content specificity. Cross-architecture transfer via ridge regression maps yields large behavioral suppression (up to 46 points) but fails specificity controls as random and orthogonal directions perform comparably. We identify a two-tier specificity structure: within-model directions are causally specific and actionable; cross-model directions are causally real but non-specific. An asymmetric transfer topology emerges, with Gemma and Qwen acting as geometric donors and Llama as a receiver. These findings define the limits of linear cross-architecture correction and recommend within-model probing for auditing.

papers.title_en: Data-Centric Benchmarking of Exploit Generation in LLMs: Understanding the Impact of Fine-Tuning
papers.authors: Yiwei Chen, Lichi Li, Kai Cheung, Vinny Parla, Ganesh Sundaram
papers.evidence: 研究微调如何影响利用生成质量，直接关系到微调导致的安全退化
papers.tldr: 针对CVE条件下的漏洞利用生成任务，现有研究缺乏标准评估和高质量数据。本文采用数据驱动方法，构建多阶段预处理的高质量数据集，并提出基于LLM-as-judge的可扩展评估框架。在统一设置下对17个大语言模型进行零样本测试，发现微调后的8B开源模型在漏洞利用质量上提升42.5%，可媲美部分闭源模型。结果表明数据质量、结构化监督和评估设计对可靠漏洞生成至关重要，其重要性不亚于模型规模。
papers.abstract_en: We study the task of CVE-conditioned exploit generation, where a model drafts proof-of-concept (PoC) exploits given software vulnerability context. We adopt a data-centric approach, constructing a high-quality dataset via multi-stage preprocessing and introducing a scalable evaluation framework with LLM-as-judge and fine-grained rubrics. Under this unified setup, we benchmark 17 large language models across 8 evaluation criteria, providing systematic insights into their zero-shot capabilities. We further show that a compact 8B open-weight model, when fine-tuned on curated data, achieves over 42.5% improvement in exploit quality and rivals some proprietary models when combined with simple test-time rejection strategies. Our results highlight the importance of data quality, structured supervision, and evaluation design for reliable exploit generation, suggesting that these factors can be as critical as model scale in adapting LLMs to cybersecurity tasks.

papers.title_en: Beyond Safe Data: Pretraining-Stage Alignment with Regular Safety Reflection
papers.authors: Jinhan Li, Kexian Tang, Yihan Xu, Zhuorui Ye, Kaifeng Lyu
papers.evidence: 提出在预训练语料中插入安全反思以保持对齐
papers.tldr: 大语言模型的安全对齐通常仅在后训练阶段进行，近期研究将安全干预提前到预训练阶段，但多聚焦于数据过滤或改写。本文指出，即使数据安全，模型仍可能从看似安全的数据中泛化出危险行为。为此提出Safety Reflection Pretraining，在预训练语料中定期插入安全反思，将自我监控直接融入语言建模。实验表明该方法显著提高安全分类准确率，降低推理和微调攻击成功率，在合成环境MedSafetyWorld中优于数据过滤和改写。研究暗示预训练对齐应超越数据安全，塑造模型从安全数据中习得的 behavior。
papers.abstract_en: To achieve deeper safety alignment for large language models (LLMs), recent efforts have studied how to push safety interventions earlier into the pretraining stage, primarily by filtering unsafe data or rewriting it into safer forms. We argue that pretraining-stage alignment should go beyond making the data safe: LLMs may compose seemingly benign knowledge and capabilities into unsafe behaviors. To this end, we propose Safety Reflection Pretraining, a pretraining-stage alignment method which regularly inserts short safety reflections into pretraining corpora to integrate self-monitoring directly into language modeling, establishing a foundational capability that is subsequently reinforced by compatible post-training. Our experiments with 1.7B models pretrained on FineWeb-Edu show that Safety Reflection Pretraining improves safety classification accuracy and substantially reduces the success rates of inference-stage and finetuning attacks. Complementary to our real-world experiments, we also introduce a fully controlled synthetic environment, MedSafetyWorld, with a clear definition of safety and a reasoning structure under which models can easily generalize unsafe behaviors from safe data. Ablations in MedSafetyWorld further demonstrate a clear advantage of Safety Reflection Pretraining in preventing models from acting on unsafe behaviors generalized from safe data, compared with data filtering and rewriting. Taken together, our findings suggest that pretraining alignment should not only make the training data safe, but also shape the behaviors that models are likely to acquire from safe data.
